source_text: I wasn't hungry because I had eaten earlier.

reference_translations.text: Non avevo fame perché avevo mangiato prima.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. avevo fame is the imperfect background; avevo mangiato is the prior action.

reference_translations.text: Non avevo fame perché ho mangiato prima.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: ho mangiato is the passato prossimo and loses the before relationship; the prior action needs the imperfect auxiliary (avevo).

explanation: The eating happened before the not-being-hungry, so Italian uses the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of avere plus the participle, avevo mangiato. The auxiliary must be the imperfect (avevo), not the present (ho), which would only give a plain passato prossimo.

source_text: She had already finished her homework when I called.

reference_translations.text: Aveva già finito i compiti quando ho chiamato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. aveva finito is the trapassato prossimo; quando ho chiamato is the later reference point.

reference_translations.text: Quando ho chiamato, aveva già finito i compiti.
reference_translations.notes: Clauses reordered; equally natural.

explanation: Finishing the homework precedes the phone call, so the earlier action takes the trapassato prossimo: aveva finito, the imperfect of avere plus the participle. già (already) reinforces the before relationship.

source_text: They had worked all day, so they were very tired.

reference_translations.text: Avevano lavorato tutto il giorno, quindi erano molto stanchi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. avevano lavorato is the prior action; erano stanchi is the resulting past state.

reference_translations.text: Hanno lavorato tutto il giorno, quindi erano molto stanchi.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: hanno lavorato is the passato prossimo; the action before the tiredness needs the imperfect auxiliary (avevano).

explanation: The working leads up to the tiredness, an action complete before another past point, so the trapassato prossimo: avevano lavorato, the imperfect of avere plus the participle. Hanno lavorato would be a plain passato prossimo.

source_text: We had already seen that film before.

reference_translations.text: Avevamo già visto quel film prima.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. visto is the irregular participle of vedere.

reference_translations.text: Avevamo già visto quel film.
reference_translations.notes: prima can be dropped; già carries the prior sense.

explanation: The watching is set before some later past moment, so the trapassato prossimo: avevamo visto, the imperfect of avere plus the irregular participle visto. The auxiliary is the imperfect (avevamo), not the present (abbiamo).

source_text: He had told the truth, but nobody believed him.

reference_translations.text: Aveva detto la verità, ma nessuno gli credette.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. detto is the irregular participle of dire; credette is passato remoto, a natural narrative past.

reference_translations.text: Aveva detto la verità, ma nessuno gli credeva.
reference_translations.notes: credeva (imperfect) is an equally natural rendering of the second clause.

explanation: Telling the truth precedes the disbelief, so the earlier action takes the trapassato prossimo: aveva detto, the imperfect of avere plus the irregular participle detto. The imperfect auxiliary (aveva) is what marks it as prior, not the present (ha).

source_text: Had you read the book before the exam?

reference_translations.text: Avevi letto il libro prima dell'esame?
reference_translations.notes: Standard. letto is the irregular participle of leggere; prima dell' makes the anteriority explicit.

reference_translations.text: Avevi già letto il libro prima dell'esame?
reference_translations.notes: già (already) optionally reinforces the prior reading.

explanation: The reading is asked about as prior to the exam, so the trapassato prossimo: avevi letto, the imperfect of avere plus the irregular participle letto. The question keeps the imperfect auxiliary (avevi), not the present (hai).

source_text: The keys? I had left them on the table.

reference_translations.text: Le chiavi? Le avevo lasciate sul tavolo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The preceding direct-object pronoun le (le chiavi, feminine plural) makes the participle agree: lasciate.

reference_translations.text: Le chiavi? Le avevo lasciato sul tavolo.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: with a preceding direct-object pronoun the participle must agree (lasciate), not stay invariable.

explanation: Leaving the keys is prior to the present mention, so the trapassato prossimo: avevo lasciato, the imperfect of avere plus the participle. Because the direct object (le chiavi) comes first as the pronoun le, the participle agrees as feminine plural, giving le avevo lasciate. The agreement rule is inherited from the passato prossimo.

source_text: When I arrived at the station, the train had already left.

reference_translations.text: Quando sono arrivato alla stazione, il treno era già partito.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. era partito is the trapassato prossimo of an essere verb; the train left before the arrival.

reference_translations.text: Quando sono arrivato alla stazione, il treno è già partito.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: è partito is the passato prossimo; the prior departure needs the imperfect auxiliary (era).

explanation: The train's departure precedes the arrival, so the trapassato prossimo. With an essere verb it is the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle: il treno (masculine singular) gives era partito. The auxiliary is the imperfect (era), not the present (è).

source_text: She had gone home before the end of the party.

reference_translations.text: Era andata a casa prima della fine della festa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. andata agrees with the feminine singular subject.

reference_translations.text: Se n'era andata a casa prima della fine della festa.
reference_translations.notes: andarsene (to go off) is an idiomatic alternative; still essere, still agreeing (andata).

explanation: Going home is prior to the end of the party, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. The feminine singular subject gives era andata. The imperfect auxiliary (era), not the present (è), marks the action as earlier.

reference_translations.text: Noi ragazze eravamo arrivate tardi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. arrivate agrees with the feminine plural subject noi ragazze.

reference_translations.text: Eravamo arrivate tardi.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun dropped; the feminine agreement still shows on arrivate.

explanation: The arriving is set before a later past reference, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. The feminine plural subject gives eravamo arrivate. The auxiliary is the imperfect (eravamo), not the present (siamo).

source_text: The children had already gone to bed.

reference_translations.text: I bambini erano già andati a letto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. andati agrees with the masculine plural subject.

reference_translations.text: I bambini se n'erano già andati a letto.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic alternative with andarsene; still essere and agreeing (andati).

explanation: Going to bed is prior to a later past moment, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. The masculine plural subject gives erano andati. The imperfect auxiliary (erano), not the present (sono), marks the earlier action.

source_text: My grandfather had been born in a small village.

reference_translations.text: Mio nonno era nato in un piccolo paese.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. nato is the irregular participle of nascere; agrees masculine singular.

reference_translations.text: Mio nonno era nato in un paesino.
reference_translations.notes: paesino (little village) is a natural diminutive alternative.

explanation: Being born is set before some later past point, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. nascere has the irregular participle nato, and the masculine singular subject gives era nato. The auxiliary is the imperfect (era), not the present (è).

source_text: The women had gone out before noon.

reference_translations.text: Le donne erano uscite prima di mezzogiorno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. uscite agrees with the feminine plural subject.

reference_translations.text: Le donne sono uscite prima di mezzogiorno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: sono uscite is the passato prossimo; the prior action needs the imperfect auxiliary (erano).

explanation: Going out is prior to noon as a past reference, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. The feminine plural subject gives erano uscite. The imperfect auxiliary (erano), not the present (sono), marks the action as earlier.

source_text: Marco and Lucia had already left when we phoned.

reference_translations.text: Marco e Lucia erano già partiti quando abbiamo telefonato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. A mixed-gender subject takes the masculine plural: partiti.

reference_translations.text: Marco e Lucia se n'erano già andati quando abbiamo telefonato.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic alternative with andarsene; mixed gender still gives masculine plural (andati).

explanation: The departure precedes the phone call, so the trapassato prossimo: the imperfect of essere plus the agreeing participle. A mixed-gender subject (a man and a woman) defaults to the masculine plural, giving erano partiti. The auxiliary is the imperfect (erano), not the present (sono).
